dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a sick person.
Hello ma'am, I'm Derek. What was the reason you called 911 today?
She fell in her room and now has pain in her right groin area.
Yes, I fell while trying to fix the bed and landed on my hip with a lot of pain.
Ms. Smith? Can you tell me your name?
Yes, Mary Smith.
Can you tell me where you are right now?
I think it's [CITY].
What is today's date, Ms. Smith?
September 29th, I believe.
Can you squeeze my finger for me?
Okay.
Despite the pain, can you move your right leg a bit?
Yes, but it's very painful.
I need to assess your breathing, Ms. Smith. Are you comfortable with that?
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
Are you noticing any shortness of breath or chest discomfort?
No, my breathing feels okay.
Radial pulse is strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 201 over 78.
Pulse is 46 and regularly irregular, with frequent premature atrial contractions.
Respiration is 20, non-labored and of normal depth.
Oxygen saturation is 96%, and the skin is warm, dry, and normal in appearance.
Ms. Smith, can you describe what happened before the fall?
I was working on my bed cover when my knee gave out and I fell hard on my hip.
Are you aware of any chronic conditions you currently manage?
Yes, I have COPD.
What medications do you currently take?
Combivent, Flovent, ProAir, and zafirlukast.
Are you allergic to any medications or foods?
I'm allergic to sulfamethoxazole and Fosamax.
How would you rate your pain today, on a scale from 0 to 10?
It's a 10. The pain is sharp and constant with any movement.
Is the pain going anywhere else in your body? Like the back or into the leg?
No, it just stays in the groin area and gets worse if I move my hip.
Does the pain change if you take a deep breath?
No, it just hurts when I move, not when I breathe deeply.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 (E4 V5 M6), indicating you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
I'll check your breathing again.
I observed increased respiratory effort, including accessory muscle use, but no shortness of breath reported.
I'll place you on 3 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula, given your COPD history and during transport considerations.
I'll check your glucose level now as a baseline measurement.
Your skin is dry, warm, and of normal appearance.
Did your fall cause any injury to your head or neck?
No head or neck injuries were involved in the fall.
I found no visible trauma to the head or neck.
Can you try to move your legs while avoiding what moves your right hip too much?
That left one is fine.
Let me check your pupils.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
Is your right leg straight or bent? Do you notice any abnormal alignment?
I haven't noticed any bends or twists in the leg.
I'm placing you on 4-lead EKG monitoring to assess for any cardiac irregularities.
I am performing a quick pass of head and neck once again.
The EKG shows a sinus rhythm with frequent premature atrial contractions.
I will now perform a 12-lead EKG to better assess your cardiac status.
The 12-lead EKG reveals bigeminy pattern, second-degree AV block (Mobitz II), and nonspecific T wave changes.
Which hospital would you prefer to go to, Ms. Smith?
I would prefer to go to [COMPANY_NAME], the closest facility.
Let me check your vital signs again.
Your blood pressure is now 151 over 84.
Your heart rate is 93, with continuing premature atrial contractions in a bigeminy pattern.
We will now carefully assist you onto the stretcher to rest in a more comfortable position.
Please don't twist or move my hip too much; it's very painful.
I will secure you using a five-point seatbelt system to keep you safe during transport.
I'll contact [COMPANY_NAME] to alert them before we arrive.
The ride to the hospital will take approximately twelve minutes, and I'll continue to monitor your condition.
Your pain remains sharp and localized to the right hip, correct?
We have confirmed no visible deformity or asymmetry in either leg, though you may have internal damage we can't see.
You're stable with no new onset symptoms or changes in condition.
Your supplemental oxygen remains at 3 liters per minute.
Feel free to grab the emergency handle on the side if anything worsens.
Ms. Smith is an 87-year-old female with right groin pain following a ground-level fall with reported impact to the hip.
She was placed on 3 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula following protocol due to her history of COPD.
EKG findings included sinus rhythm with premature atrial contractions; however, some nonperfusing PACs/PJCs were identified.
She denied any head or neck trauma, weakness, vision changes, or pain with breathing.
Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation with full limb motor capability aside from right hip discomfort.
I observed increased respiratory effort with accessory muscle use, but no reported dyspnea.
Her blood pressure decreased during transport to 151/84 with a heart rate of 93 and progressing premature atrial contractions and Mobitz II noted.
No abnormal findings in airway, breathing, or circulation, and no pain with inspiration or expiration reported by the patient.
Ms. Smith is A&O x4 and in no distress.
No abnormal findings on skin exam, though no STEMI or STEVIs were identified.
Ms. Smith was transported in a pain-relieving position with use of five-point seatbelt restraint for safety.
We followed the general trauma management (Protocol 4 - 1) for this case.
She remains comfortable with no pain from breathing and vitals continuing to be stable during transport.
Your blood sugar was 0 at baseline per protocol, but no hypoglycemic symptoms were reported.
No signs of further deformity, dislocation, or asymmetry or limb exams.
No chest discomfort or shortness of breath was noted, per patient statement.
As no head injury signs were evident, only palpable trauma to the hip were reported by the patient. She was medically cleared to transport.
